turns.content: I'm trying to understand how to split a stream of sensor readings into two separate vectors, one for values above a threshold and another for the rest. I've heard you can use an iterator method for this instead of collecting everything first.

You are looking at the partition method on iterators which takes a predicate closure and yields two iterators: one containing elements where your predicate returns true, and another for false. This avoids materializing the full collection in memory before splitting it. If you want to group adjacent identical readings instead of filtering by value, you would use the group_by method which groups consecutive equal items into tuples of (key, iterator).

That makes sense for partitioning, but I'm running into an edge case where my closure returns true only on even indices, and I need to know exactly how many elements went into each bucket without storing all of them. Can you explain the difference between using partition with a simple predicate versus using group_by when my data has runs of identical values?

Partition simply forwards every element that matches your closure to one output stream and ignores it for the other, so if your predicate depends on index you cannot use it directly unless you wrap the iterator in something like a Peekable or collect into a Vec first. Group_by is specifically designed to group adjacent equal elements into a vector of (key, iterator) tuples where each key repeats until the next distinct value appears; this is useful when you want to compress consecutive duplicates rather than filter by condition.
